steps.prompt: How many finite verbs are in this sentence?

hints.one: 'Surrexit': 'Surrexit' is one finite verb, but 'apparuit' is also finite (perfect indicative)
hints.one: 'apparuit': 'Apparuit' is one finite verb, but 'Surrexit' is also finite
hints.three: There are exactly two finite verbs in this sentence

hints.to stretch: 'To stretch' is 'surgere' is not 'to stretch' — it means 'to rise'

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'surgit' — 'Surrexit' with -exit is perfect
hints.future perfect: Future perfect would be 'surrexerit' — 'Surrexit' is perfect

hints.rego, regere, rexi, rectum: These are the parts of 'rego' (to rule/guide)
hints.erigo, erigere, erexi, erectum: These are the parts of 'erigo' (to raise up)
hints.resurgo, resurgere, resurrexi, resurrect: These are the parts of 'resurgo' (to rise again) — related but different compound

hints.to prepare: 'To prepare' is 'parare/apparare' — 'apparuit' comes from 'apparere' (ad- + parere, to appear)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'apparet' — 'apparuit' with -uit is perfect

hints.paro, parare, paravi, paratum: These are the parts of 'paro' (to prepare)
hints.pareo, parere, parui, paritum: These are the parts of 'pareo' (to obey) — 'apparere' is ad- + parere (to appear)
hints.apparo, apparare, apparavi, apparatum: These are the parts of 'apparo' (to prepare) — different from 'apparere' (to appear)

steps.reveal: Simoni = to Simon (dat., indirect object of 'apparuit')

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Simonis' — 'Simoni' with -i is dative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Simonis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Simonis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'Simonis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What is the position and effect of 'vere' in this sentence?
steps.target: adverb placed emphatically between subject and verb: 'truly/indeed'
choices: adverb placed emphatically between subject and verb: 'truly/indeed'
steps.reveal: Surrexit Dominus vere = The Lord has truly risen

hints.adjective modifying 'Dominus': 'Vere' is an adverb (truly), not an adjective — the adjective would be 'verus'
hints.conjunction joining the clauses: 'Et' is the conjunction — 'vere' is an emphatic adverb
hints.noun meaning 'spring': 'Ver' (spring) has ablative 'vere', but here 'vere' is the adverb 'truly'

translation: The Lord has truly risen and has appeared to Simon
